steps.title: Compute the confidence interval for the population mean
steps.content: Mean ± margin of error = $4.5 \pm 0.5$, giving the interval $[4,\,5]$ miles. This interval estimates where the true population mean lies, not where individual hikers' distances fall.

steps.title: Understand what margin of error does NOT tell us
steps.content: The margin of error says nothing about individual visitors' distances. It cannot guarantee every visitor, or even most visitors, hiked within that range. It also cannot rule out any individual hiking any particular distance.

steps.title: Select the best conclusion
steps.content: The interval $[4, 5]$ is a plausible range for the mean distance hiked by all visitors. That matches choice D — D is correct.

choiceAnalysis:
• Choice A — 'It is likely that all visitors hiked between 4 and 5 miles.': Incorrectly applies the mean's confidence interval to individual hikers. Individual distances can vary widely around any mean. ✗
  • Choice B — 'It is likely that most visitors hiked exactly 4.5 miles.': The sample mean is an average, not a typical individual value; most hikers need not have hiked exactly the mean distance. ✗
  • Choice C — 'It is not possible that any visitor hiked less than 3 miles.': Uses absolute language ('not possible') and misapplies the interval to individuals. The margin of error says nothing about minimum individual distances. ✗
  • Choice D — 'It is plausible that the mean distance hiked for all visitors is between 4 and 5 miles.': Correctly identifies that the confidence interval $[4, 5]$ gives a plausible range for the population mean, not individual distances. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
